description: Either way, there is still a choice to be made.

hints.text: The goal is $(A \lor B) \to (A \lor (B \lor C))$. No matter which disjunct holds — $A$ or $B$ — you can build the broader [conclusion|conclusion] $A \lor (B \lor C)$. Proof by cases is the natural strategy. Start by assuming the left side.
From $A$, you can form $A \lor (B \lor C)$ directly — $A$ sits on the left of a big $\lor$, and the right side can be anything you like (in this case, $B \lor C$). Use `or-intro-l`.
From $B$, first form $B \lor C$ using `or-intro-l` with $B$ on the left and $C$ on the right.
Now you have $B \lor C$. Use `or-intro-r` to place $A$ on the left, giving $A \lor (B \lor C)$. Two different paths, same destination.

Time for proof by cases. Step 1 is your [disjunction|disjunction] $A \lor B$. Steps 3 and 6 both prove $A \lor (B \lor C)$ — one from assuming $A$, the other from assuming $B$. Use `or-elim` to conclude.
